Found in most coastal basins from Vinh to Thuy Hoa, except from the basins of the rivers Huong and Quang tri, which enter the sea at the city of Hue and Dong Ha, respectively. Also recorded in headwaters of the Dong Nai River, which enter the sea at Saigon, in headwaters of the Sesan River, a tributary of the Sekong River, itself a tributary of the Mekong River. Apparently, the distribution in rivers Ba, Dong Nai, and Sesan is partly the result of introductions; recorded only in mountainous regions (Ref. 42924). Present in Da River (Ref. 90167, 89724). Known from the Mekong River. Undertakes upstream migration beginning in November when the water level within the river decreases and continues well into the dry seaon, at least until February. In the late dry season, or the start of the monsoon season, a downstream migration takes place from the Khone Falls to the Mekong Delta (Ref. 37770). Also Ref. 37770, 43281.

Known from the Mekong Delta at Hong Ngu in Dong Thap Province and  at Thot Not in Can Tho Province. Juveniles of 2 cm appear in June and the end of the year they have grown to about 20 cm. Also Ref. 43281, 49196.

Known from the Mekong basin. Migrates downstream from May to August from Stung Treng to Kandal in Cambodia and further into the Mekong Delta in Viet Nam, at least to Cai Be. This downstream migration is both a spawning and a trophic migration eventually bringing the fish onto floodplain areas during the flood season. At Ang Giang and Dong Thap Provinces, larvae occur every year in June-July during their downstream drift from spawning site somewhere upstream in Cambodia. They are fished in specialized larvae dai nets just south of the Cambodian-Viet Namese border and are used as stocking materials in the  cage culture industry. Fish larvae of 2 cm are reported in May-July. Generally, fish from the Mekong Delta are below 50 cm, dominated by fish below 30 cm (Ref. 37770). Also Ref. 49196.

Known from rivers along the Viet Nam coast (Ref. 12693). Recorded from the River Hue, Annam (Ref. 7432). Found in Southern Vietnam (Ref. 9706). Occurs sympatrically with P. kunyit in the Mekong delta (Ref. 33567). Undertakes upstream migration in November-February and downstream migration from April to July at Cao Lanh in Dong Thap Province of the Mekong basin, both movements involving mainly big fishes of 70 to 90 cm (Ref. 37770). Also distributed in brackish water (Ref. 49196). Also Ref. 12369, 49196.

Found in high altitudes in Rivers Ko and Tan near Nha Trang, Annam.  This has not been rediscovered since the collection of the types in 1936 ( Ref. 40772); even with extensive surveys done in 2000 and 2002 in the river, it was still not collected that the known populations are considered to be possibly extinct.  Assessed as Critically Endangered in August 11, 2010, with 0-49 mature individuals, with extent of occurrence at 100-120 km², and an ongoing decline in quality of habitat. Further survey is needed within the type locality and possible range to confirm the continued presence and should be reassessed if further populations are found (Ref. 117882). .Also Ref. 12041, 26580, 39226.

It is common in fish markets of the city of Nha Trang and is edible (Ref. 79889).

Known from the Ea Krong No drainage basin, composed of montane mixed pine and evergreen forest between 596-1112m, in Lam Dong Province.  Harvested by local people using cast nets during dry season from December to April when river water levels are low. This method of harvesting is  unlikely to affect its populations (Ref. 102622).

Known from the Da Teh catchment of middle Dong Nai drainage in evergreen forest between 140−200 m. Likely to be confined to relatively small stretches of low-elevation forest streams of the middle Dong Nai drainage and thus particularly vulnerable to threats such as siltation and overfishing.  Common human activities such as modification of stream morphology, logging, deforestation, agriculture and overfishing in the Da Teh catchment potentially pose threat to its population (Ref. 102622).
Status of threat: data deficient following IUCN Red List categories (IUCN 2014,
version 11) (Ref. 102622).
